high_value_elements: Correctly identifies full mu agonists (morphine, fentanyl, hydromorphone, methadone) and distinguishes from partial agonist (buprenorphine) and kappa agonist (butorphanol)
Correctly states NSAIDs work via COX enzyme inhibition; distinguishes COX-1 vs COX-2 selectivity and GI safety implications
Correctly identifies grapiprant as an EP4 receptor antagonist, not a COX inhibitor
Correctly identifies ketamine CRI (~60 mg/L at 5-10 mL/kg/hr) and amantadine (3-5 mg/kg once daily orally) as the two primary NMDA antagonist options with different routes and clinical contexts

Correctly identifies tramadol: M1 (O-desmethyltramadol) as the active mu-opioid metabolite; CYP2D6 required for conversion; many dogs have limited CYP2D6 activity making tramadol act primarily as SNRI; cats have effective analgesic response at 2 mg/kg TID
Correctly identifies anti-NGF monoclonal antibodies: frunevetmab (Solensia) for cats, bedinvetmab (Librela) for dogs; mechanism is NGF blockade preventing TrkA receptor activation
States acetaminophen is absolutely contraindicated in cats due to glucuronidation deficiency causing methemoglobinemia
major_concerns: Does not know tramadol's M1 metabolite or the CYP2D6 species difference
Cannot explain the feline NSAID caution beyond 'cats are sensitive to drugs'
Confuses corticosteroid and NSAID mechanism of action

high_value_elements: Correctly avoids long-term NSAIDs in IRIS Stage 3-4 CKD cat (prostaglandin E2 required for afferent arteriolar tone)
Includes Solensia (frunevetmab) as a preferred option for this CKD cat: renal-safe mechanism, monthly subcutaneous injection, specifically licensed for feline OA pain
Selects buprenorphine via oral transmucosal (OTM) route as the practical home opioid for cats
Includes gabapentin with explicit dose reduction for Stage 3-4 CKD: half-life extends from ~4 hours in normal cats to 13-20 hours in Stage 3-4 (linear correlation with IRIS stage)
Adds amantadine 3-5 mg/kg once daily for the neuropathic pain flare component
Correctly administers zoledronate: 0.1-0.5 mg/kg IV over exactly 15 minutes in normal saline only (not dextrose), maximum 4 mg; pre-screens for active dental disease
major_concerns: Prescribes long-term NSAID in Stage 3 CKD without contraindication acknowledgment
Does not recognize gabapentin half-life extension in CKD and prescribes standard dosing intervals
Combines NSAID and corticosteroid without contraindication awareness
Administers zoledronate in dextrose or without checking for dental disease

high_value_elements: Immediately flags the NSAID plus corticosteroid combination as requiring intervention (dual prostaglandin inhibition and mucosal barrier disruption)
Identifies bisphosphonate dental disease contraindication: active dental disease or planned procedures require washout due to jaw osteonecrosis risk
Correctly describes DEA Schedule II/III record-keeping requirements: drug log, biannual inventory, separate locked storage, disposal records
major_concerns: Does not recognize the NSAID plus corticosteroid combination as dangerous
Cannot describe any controlled substance compliance requirement
Does not know the bisphosphonate dental disease contraindication

high_value_elements: Explains GI hemorrhage mechanism of NSAID + corticosteroid: NSAIDs reduce gastroprotective prostaglandins; corticosteroids reduce mucosal repair and increase acid secretion
Explains renal hemodynamic mechanism of NSAID risk in CKD: afferent arteriolar tone is prostaglandin-dependent when renal perfusion pressure is compromised
Justifies buprenorphine over full mu for home use: ceiling effect on respiratory depression, practical OTM absorption in cats (~50% bioavailability via OTM)
Justifies Solensia as renal-safe: mechanism does not involve renal prostaglandin pathways
major_concerns: Provides drug names without pharmacologic justification
Cannot explain the mechanism behind any contraindication

high_value_elements: Acknowledges no NSAID is risk-free in CKD and articulates a monitoring plan if short-term use is considered
Recognizes tramadol's limited evidence for analgesia in dogs and does not recommend it as a sole analgesic in that species
Acknowledges uncertainty about optimal gabapentin dosing in CKD cats and commits to starting low with clinical monitoring
major_concerns: Presents drugs as safe in contexts where the fellowship explicitly identified risks
Cannot identify a single limitation or monitoring requirement for any drug selected

high_value_elements: Constructs a specific opioid-sparing TPLO protocol: NSAID + gabapentinoid + amantadine or ketamine CRI + locoregional block (e.g., femoral/sciatic nerve block)
Integrates Epstein fellowship content: duloxetine ~1 mg/kg q24h orally for dogs for neuropathic pain; amitriptyline 0.5-2 mg/kg q12-24h dogs or 5-10 mg per cat once daily
Discusses CBD evidence critically: starting dose 0.1-2 mg/kg BID; notes THC is toxic to pets; requires product purity verification
major_concerns: Cannot construct a realistic opioid-sparing protocol with specific drugs and doses
Discusses pharmacology only in abstract terms disconnected from clinical decision-making
